claim: The phonetic word generator is a 4th-order Markov / n-gram letter-transition model — the TransitionTableBuilder is constructed with order=4 over the alphabet plus the space char: new TransitionTableBuilder(4, [0x0020, ...alphabet]).
correctedStatement:
CONFIRMED literally: in packages/keybr-generators/lib/generate-languages.ts, the generator builds its phonetic model with exactly `const builder = new TransitionTableBuilder(4, [0x0020, ...alphabet]);` — order=4, alphabet prefixed with the space code point 0x0020. The model is built from the top-10,000 unique words of each frequency dictionary (`sortByCount(language, dict).slice(0, 10000)`), restricted to words of length >= 3, and weighted by frequency (each word is appended `count` times). So "order=4 over the space char + alphabet, from the top-10,000 dictionary words weighted by frequency" is correct.

PARTIALLY CORRECT on terminology: "4th-order Markov" is imprecise. The argument `4` is the chain/tuple width, making this a 4-gram (n=4) letter model in which each next letter is conditioned on the previous 3 letters. In strict Markov terminology that is a 3rd-ORDER Markov chain (memory = order - 1 = 3 letters), not a 4th-order one. The code reflects this: in builder.ts, `Chain` keeps a sliding window `new Array(order).fill(0x0020)` of length 4, and in transitiontable.ts `Chain.segments = size^(order-1)` (= size^3 contexts) with `Chain.width = size^order`. So call it a "4-gram (order=4 tuple) letter-transition model = 3rd-order Markov chain," not "4th-order Markov."

The "2nd-order Markov chain" description is REFUTED as the generation order: PhoneticModel.ngram1()/ngram2() (backed by TransitionTable.toNgram1()/toNgram2()) are derived marginalized unigram/bigram letter-frequency views used elsewhere (e.g. keyboard heat coloring), NOT the order used to generate words. Word generation in phoneticmodel.ts (nextWord) indexes the full order-4 chain via table.segment(word). The flag's spot-check is therefore vindicated: order=4 (the literal constructor argument) is correct; "2nd-order" was a misread of the ngram1/ngram2 accessor methods.
evidence:
PRIMARY SOURCE 1 — packages/keybr-generators/lib/generate-languages.ts (fetched via GitHub API, master branch). Imports `import { TransitionTableBuilder } from "@keybr/phonetic-model";`. In generateModel(dict): `const builder = new TransitionTableBuilder(4, [0x0020, ...alphabet]);` then `for (const [word, count] of dict) { if (word.length >= 3) { for (let i = 0; i < count; i++) { builder.append(language.lowerCase(word)); } } }` then `const table = builder.build();`. Upstream: `const slice = sortByCount(language, dict).slice(0, 10000); generateModel(slice);` — confirms top-10,000 words, frequency-weighted via the count loop. Output written to ../keybr-phonetic-model/assets/model-${id}.data via table.compress().

PRIMARY SOURCE 2 — packages/keybr-phonetic-model/lib/builder.ts. `export class TransitionTableBuilder { constructor(order: number, alphabet: readonly CodePoint[]) { this.chain = new Chain(order, alphabet); this.frequencies = new Float64Array(this.chain.width); } ... append(word: string): void { const { order, alphabet } = this.chain; const chain = new Array(order).fill(0x0020); ... } }`. The sliding-window `push()` shifts the array and appends each code point; with order=4 the window holds 4 code points (3 context + 1 next).

PRIMARY SOURCE 3 — packages/keybr-phonetic-model/lib/transitiontable.ts. `class Chain { constructor(order, alphabet) { this.size = this.alphabet.length; this.segments = Math.pow(this.size, this.order - 1); this.width = Math.pow(this.size, this.order); ... } }` — segments = size^(order-1) = size^3 contexts, confirming each segment is keyed by a 3-letter prefix. toNgram1()/toNgram2() build marginal unigram/bigram tables FROM the order-4 table (derived views).

PRIMARY SOURCE 4 — packages/keybr-phonetic-model/lib/phoneticmodel.ts. PhoneticModel exposes abstract ngram1()/ngram2(); makePhoneticModel implements them as `return table.toNgram1();` / `return table.toNgram2();` — i.e. derived views, not the generation order. Generation (nextWord) uses `table.segment(word)` over the full order-4 chain, with space (0x0020) boosted by Math.pow(1.3, word.length) to shorten words; minLength=3, maxLength=10.

claim: A new letter is unlocked in GUIDED mode only when every currently-included key has reached confidence ≥ 1 — by default using each key's BEST historical confidence (bestConfidence), so you only need to hit target speed once; the 'recoverKeys' toggle (default false) switches the gate to current confidence.
evidence: Primary source, packages/keybr-lesson/lib/guided.ts (GuidedLesson.update): `const recoverKeys = this.settings.get(lessonProps.guided.recoverKeys);` ... per-iteration `if ((lessonKey.bestConfidence ?? 0) >= 1) { // Must include all confident keys. lessonKeys.include(lessonKey.letter); continue; }` then `if (recoverKeys) { if (includedKeys.every((key) => (key.confidence ?? 0) >= 1)) { // Include a new key only when all the previous keys are now above the target speed. } } else { if (includedKeys.every((key) => (key.bestConfidence ?? 0) >= 1)) { // Include a new key only when all the previous keys were once above the target speed. } }`. Default: packages/keybr-lesson/lib/settings.ts `recoverKeys: booleanProp("lesson.guided.recoverKeys", false)`. Confidence definition: packages/keybr-lesson/lib/target.ts `return speedToTime(this.targetSpeed) / timeToType;` and packages/keybr-lesson/lib/key.ts `confidence: target.confidence(timeToType), bestConfidence: target.confidence(bestTimeToType)` (bestConfidence derived from bestTimeToType). UI label (packages/page-practice/lib/settings/lesson/RecoverKeysProp.tsx): "Unlock a next key only when: The previous keys are also above the target speed" with description "If this option is disabled, you unlock a new key by raising only the focused key above the target speed. If this option is enabled, you will have to raise the focused key and all the previous keys above the target speed." Decisive unit test (packages/keybr-lesson/lib/guided.test.ts) "the unlocked key has no confidence level / all previous keys were once above the target speed": input [letter1, 0.9, 1] for A–F (current=0.9, best=1); recover off → "ABCDEF[G]J" (new key unlocked), recover on → "[A]BCDEFJ" (no new key, re-focus weakest). Fetched via authenticated gh API (raw GitHub web search returned HTTP 429).

claim: Per-key 'confidence' = speedToTime(targetSpeed) / timeToType, so confidence >= 1 means at/above target speed; it is a pure speed ratio, NOT a sample-count or statistical-certainty measure.
correctedStatement: In the keybr.com source (github.com/aradzie/keybr.com, master branch), per-key "confidence" is defined in packages/keybr-lesson/lib/target.ts as Target.confidence(timeToType) returning exactly `speedToTime(this.targetSpeed) / timeToType`. speedToTime (packages/keybr-result/lib/result.ts) converts a speed in characters-per-minute into a time-per-keystroke in milliseconds (`return 1000 / (v / 60)`), and timeToType is also a time-per-keystroke in milliseconds (the smoothed/filtered average time to type the key, measured by TimeToType in packages/keybr-textinput-events/lib/timetotype.ts). Therefore confidence = targetTimePerKey / actualTimePerKey = actualSpeed / targetSpeed: a pure, dimensionless SPEED ratio. confidence >= 1 exactly means your time-per-keystroke is at or below the target time, i.e. you are typing that key at or above the target speed. It is NOT statistical confidence and NOT a sample count: in packages/keybr-lesson/lib/key.ts the per-key `samples` array (sample count) is carried as a completely separate field and is not folded into the confidence value. The guided-lesson logic (packages/keybr-lesson/lib/guided.ts) confirms the semantics: a key counts as learned when `(key.confidence ?? 0) >= 1` for all included keys, and keys with `confidence < 1` are the ones still drilled (sorted ascending by confidence). One precision nit: timeToType is a smoothed/filtered running average of per-keystroke times (an exponential filter in packages/keybr-result/lib/keystats.ts), not a single raw sample, but it remains purely a timing/speed quantity with no statistical-certainty component. The original claim is accurate.
evidence: PRIMARY SOURCE (raw GitHub, aradzie/keybr.com master). (1) packages/keybr-lesson/lib/target.ts: `confidence(timeToType: number | null): number | null { if (timeToType == null) return null; if (!Number.isFinite(timeToType) || timeToType === 0) throw new Error(); return speedToTime(this.targetSpeed) / timeToType; }` and `this.targetSpeed = settings.get(lessonProps.targetSpeed)`. (2) packages/keybr-result/lib/result.ts lines ~100-122: `/** Convert typing speed in characters per minute to type-to-type in milliseconds. */ export function speedToTime(v: number): number { ... return 1000 / (v / 60); }` and its inverse `export function timeToSpeed(v): number { ... return (60 * 1000) / v; }` documented as 'Convert type-to-type in milliseconds to typing speed in characters per minute.' Both numerator and denominator are thus times-per-keystroke in ms, making the ratio dimensionless = actualSpeed/targetSpeed. (3) packages/keybr-textinput-events/lib/timetotype.ts: class TimeToType 'Measures the time taken to type a character... return duration / (size + 1)' (duration in ms, divided by number of modifier keys + 1) — pure timing, no statistics. (4) packages/keybr-lesson/lib/key.ts lines 7-15: `const { letter, samples, timeToType, bestTimeToType } = keyStats; return new LessonKey({ letter, samples, timeToType, bestTimeToType, confidence: target.confidence(timeToType), bestConfidence: target.confidence(bestTimeToType) });` — `samples` (sample count) and `confidence` are separate fields; confidence is derived only from timeToType. (5) packages/keybr-lesson/lib/guided.ts line 79: `if (includedKeys.every((key) => (key.confidence ?? 0) >= 1))`; lines 96-102: `confidenceOf(key) ...; .filter((key) => confidenceOf(key) < 1).sort((a, b) => confidenceOf(a) - confidenceOf(b))` — confirms >= 1 == learned/at-target, < 1 == still below target. (6) packages/keybr-result/lib/keystats.ts: timeToType is a filtered running value (`const filteredTimeToType = this.#filter.add(timeToType); ... this.#timeToType = filteredTimeToType;`) kept distinct from `samples`. The keybr.com /help page is JS-rendered and returned no usable text via WebFetch, so the code is the authoritative source.

claim: Default target speed is 175 internal units = characters-per-minute (CPM), which equals 35 WPM (WPM = CPM/5, i.e. 5 chars per word); adjustable 75–750 CPM (15–150 WPM).
correctedStatement:
Confirmed and accurate. In github.com/aradzie/keybr.com, the default lesson target speed is 175, with min 75 and max 750, defined in packages/keybr-lesson/lib/settings.ts: `targetSpeed: numberProp("lesson.targetSpeed", 175, { min: 75, max: 750 })`. The internal unit is CPM (characters per minute): in packages/keybr-result/lib/result.ts the function timeToSpeed returns (60*1000)/v with the docstring "Typing speed in characters per minute," and in packages/keybr-result/lib/speedunit.ts SpeedUnit.CPM has factor 1 while SpeedUnit.WPM has factor 1/5 (the 5-chars-per-word convention). Conversion is applied by `measure = (cpm) => cpm * this.factor` and used in packages/keybr-lesson-ui/lib/format.ts via `speedUnit.measure(value)`. Therefore 175 CPM × 1/5 = 35 WPM, 75 CPM = 15 WPM, and 750 CPM = 150 WPM. One clarification: the stored/internal value is always CPM; whether the user SEES CPM or WPM (or WPS/CPS) depends on the separately-configured display speedUnit (uiProps.speedUnit), and the slider in TargetSpeedProp.tsx ranges over the raw CPM min/max (75–750) with the prev/next step buttons snapping to multiples of 5.</correctedStatement>
<parameter name="evidence">Primary source from the official repository github.com/aradzie/keybr.com (fetched via GitHub API). (1) packages/keybr-lesson/lib/settings.ts line 60: `targetSpeed: numberProp("lesson.targetSpeed", 175, { min: 75, max: 750 }),` — default 175, min 75, max 750. (2) packages/keybr-result/lib/speedunit.ts: `static readonly WPM = new SpeedUnit("wpm", 1 / 5, ...)`, `static readonly CPM = new SpeedUnit("cpm", 1, ...)`, with `measure = (cpm: number): number => { return cpm * this.factor; }` — the parameter is explicitly named cpm, confirming the internal unit is characters-per-minute and the WPM divisor is 5 (factor 1/5). (3) packages/keybr-result/lib/result.ts lines 104-111: docstring "@return Typing speed in characters per minute." and `timeToSpeed(v) => (60*1000)/v` confirms internal speed is CPM. (4) packages/keybr-lesson-ui/lib/format.ts: `formatSpeed` computes `formatNumber(speedUnit.measure(value), opts)` so 175 → 175*(1/5)=35 under WPM. (5) packages/page-practice/lib/settings/lesson/TargetSpeedProp.tsx: Range `min={lessonProps.targetSpeed.min} max={lessonProps.targetSpeed.max}` (75–750) and label "Target typing speed:". Computed: 175/5=35 WPM, 75/5=15 WPM, 750/5=150 WPM — all match the claim exactly.

claim: Per-key speed is exponentially smoothed (EMA) across lessons with alpha = 0.1, and bestTimeToType is the running minimum of the SMOOTHED (filtered) values, not the raw per-lesson times.
correctedStatement: Confirmed against the open-source keybr.com frontend (github.com/aradzie/keybr.com). In packages/keybr-result/lib/keystats.ts, the MutableKeyStats class creates its per-key filter with makeFilter(0.1), so the smoothing factor alpha = 0.1. The filter (packages/keybr-math/lib/filter.ts) is explicitly an exponential moving average — its JSDoc cites en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exponential_smoothing and its add(v) implements value = alpha * v + (1 - alpha) * value (the first sample seeds value = v). On each lesson result, append() computes filteredTimeToType = this.#filter.add(timeToType), sets this.#timeToType = filteredTimeToType, and updates this.#bestTimeToType = Math.min(this.#bestTimeToType ?? Infinity, filteredTimeToType). Thus bestTimeToType is the running minimum over the SMOOTHED (filtered) per-key values, not over the raw per-lesson timeToType. The raw timeToType is retained inside each KeySample but is not what the minimum is taken over. One precision note: the EMA accumulates across results/lessons in the order they are fed into makeKeyStatsMap, and the very first sample is passed through unfiltered (value = v) before smoothing begins. Caveat: this is verifiable only for the published open-source frontend; whether the live production deployment uses an identical alpha (vs a server-tuned value) cannot be independently confirmed.
evidence: PRIMARY SOURCE 1 — packages/keybr-result/lib/keystats.ts (fetched via GitHub API, decoded from base64). MutableKeyStats constructor: `this.#filter = makeFilter(0.1);`. The append() method: `const filteredTimeToType = this.#filter.add(timeToType);` ... `this.#timeToType = filteredTimeToType;` and `this.#bestTimeToType = Math.min(this.#bestTimeToType ?? Infinity, filteredTimeToType,);`. The KeySample type stores both `timeToType` (raw) and `filteredTimeToType` (smoothed). Only filteredTimeToType is used for both timeToType and the bestTimeToType minimum. PRIMARY SOURCE 2 — packages/keybr-math/lib/filter.ts: JSDoc `@see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exponential_smoothing`; `export const makeFilter = (alpha: number): Filter => { let n = 0; let value = NaN; return { ... add: (v) => { n++; if (n > 1) { value = alpha * v + (1 - alpha) * value; } else { value = v; } return value; } }; }`. This is a textbook EMA where alpha is the smoothing factor and the first sample initializes the state. PRIMARY SOURCE 3 — packages/keybr-math/lib/filter.test.ts confirms the numerical behavior with makeFilter(0.5): add(1),add(1),add(1)=1, then add(5)=3, add(5)=4, add(5)=4.5, matching alpha*v+(1-alpha)*prev. File paths located via GitHub code search (gh api search/code) which returned packages/keybr-result/lib/keystats.ts for bestTimeToType and packages/keybr-math/lib/filter.ts for makeFilter.

claim: Weak-key targeting: the single least-confident included key with confidence < 1 becomes the 'focused' key, and generated lesson text is biased toward it via a Filter(includedKeys, focusedKey) — the focused letter is forced into words through prefix seeding while only unlocked letters are ever emitted.
correctedStatement: Verified against primary source (github.com/aradzie/keybr.com, master branch). The claim is accurate in every part. (1) Focused-key selection in packages/keybr-lesson/lib/guided.ts: `const weakestKeys = lessonKeys.findIncludedKeys().filter((key) => confidenceOf(key) < 1).sort((a, b) => confidenceOf(a) - confidenceOf(b));` followed by `if (weakestKeys.length > 0) { lessonKeys.focus(weakestKeys[0].letter); }` — i.e., the single least-confident INCLUDED key whose confidence is strictly < 1 (sorted ascending, index [0]) becomes the focused key. (2) Bias mechanism in guided.ts: `const filter = new Filter(lessonKeys.findIncludedKeys(), lessonKeys.findFocusedKey());` is built and passed into the word generator (`this.#makeWordGenerator(filter, rng)`), exactly the Filter(includedKeys, focusedKey) signature described. (3) The Filter class (packages/keybr-phonetic-model/lib/filter.ts) holds `codePoints: CodePointSet | null` (the included/unlocked letters) and `focusedCodePoint: CodePoint | null`; `includes(codePoint)` returns `this.codePoints == null || this.codePoints.has(codePoint)`, and the constructor requires the focused letter to be a member of the letters array. (4) Prefix-forcing in packages/keybr-phonetic-model/lib/phoneticmodel.ts: `findPrefixes(filter)` reads `focusedCodePoint` from the filter and returns matching pre-computed prefixes, falling back to `[new Prefix([focusedCodePoint])]` when none match — guaranteeing the focused letter can appear. In `nextWord`, the word is seeded with a prefix via `word.push(...randomSample(prefixes, random).codePoints)`, then the generation loop appends further code points but rejects any non-space code point where `!filter.includes(codePoint)`, so only unlocked/included letters are emitted (spaces are gated separately by minLength). The only nuance worth noting: 'forced into words through prefix seeding' is precise — the focused letter enters at the word's beginning via the prefix; the rest of the word is constrained (not forced to the focused letter) to the included-letter set.
evidence: guided.ts (packages/keybr-lesson/lib): weakestKeys = findIncludedKeys().filter(key => confidenceOf(key) < 1).sort((a,b) => confidenceOf(a) - confidenceOf(b)); then lessonKeys.focus(weakestKeys[0].letter); and const filter = new Filter(lessonKeys.findIncludedKeys(), lessonKeys.findFocusedKey()); passed to the word generator. filter.ts (keybr-phonetic-model/lib): constructor(letters0, focused0) -> stores codePoints (Set of included letters) and focusedCodePoint; includes(codePoint) => codePoints == null || codePoints.has(codePoint); focused letter must exist within letters. phoneticmodel.ts (keybr-phonetic-model/lib): findPrefixes(filter) { const { focusedCodePoint } = filter; if (focusedCodePoint != null) { const prefixes = this.map.get(focusedCodePoint)!.filter(p => p.matches(filter)); if (prefixes.length > 0) return prefixes; else return [new Prefix([focusedCodePoint])]; } else return []; }. nextWord(filter, random): prefixes = prefixList.findPrefixes(filter); word.push(...randomSample(prefixes, random).codePoints); then loop: table.segment(word).filter(({codePoint}) => { if (codePoint === 0x0020) { if (word.length < minLength) return false; } else { if (!filter.includes(codePoint)) return false; } return true; }).

claim: keybr remaining-lessons prediction fits polynomial regression degree 1/2/3 by sample count over last 30 samples, requires R2 at least 0.5, derivative at last index is learning rate, scans up to 50 lessons ahead.
correctedStatement: Confirmed verbatim against primary source packages/keybr-lesson/lib/learningrate.ts (class LearningRate) in github.com/aradzie/keybr.com master. All literals match the claim. LearningRate.from uses samples.slice(-30), the last 30 samples. getPolynomialDegree returns 3 cubic when length greater than 20, 2 quadratic when length greater than 10, else 1 linear. The gate is certainty greater-or-equal 0.5 where certainty is r2 of the fit. learningRate is mSpeed.derivative().eval(lastIndex) with lastIndex equal to samples[length-1].index plus 1. A loop runs i from 1 to 50 inclusive and sets remainingLessons to the first i where mSpeed.eval(lastIndex+i) reaches target.targetSpeed; if none within 50 it stays NaN. One added precision: before the regression, findSession can trim the up-to-30 samples to the latest uninterrupted learning session (no gap over one hour 3600000 ms, and trimming a speed-regression run of length at least 5), and the polynomial degree is chosen from that session length, so the effective count may be under 30. Last commit ec590fb dated 2023-11-09.
evidence: learningrate.ts: static from uses const recent = samples.slice(-30). Constructor: session = findSession(samples), samples replaced if hasData, mSpeed = polynomialRegression(vIndex, vSpeed, getPolynomialDegree(length)), certainty = r2(vIndex, vSpeed, mSpeed), guarded by certainty greater-or-equal 0.5, lastIndex = samples[length-1].index + 1, learningRate = mSpeed.derivative().eval(lastIndex), loop i = 1 to 50 setting remainingLessons = i when mSpeed.eval(lastIndex+i) greater-or-equal target.targetSpeed. getPolynomialDegree: length greater than 20 returns 3, greater than 10 returns 2, else 1. findSession in learningsession.ts trims on gap over 3600000 ms or regression run length at least 5. Last commit ec590fb dated 2023-11-09T13:44:01Z, path confirmed via gh api search/code.

claim: Default keybr.com text-input behavior does NOT end or hard-stop a lesson on error: stopOnError=true (cursor pauses until the correct key), forgiveErrors=true (auto-recovers a single wrong/skipped char via 3-char lookahead), and spaceSkipsWords=true are all on by default.
correctedStatement:
In keybr.com's open-source frontend (github.com/aradzie/keybr.com, packages/keybr-textinput/lib/settings.ts), the user-facing default settings — defined by `textInputProps` and read via `toTextInputSettings()` — are: stopOnError = TRUE, forgiveErrors = TRUE, but spaceSkipsWords = FALSE (not true). So only TWO of the three claimed defaults are correct. The claim's "spaceSkipsWords=true on by default" is REFUTED for the live/user defaults. (There is a separate static constant `textInputSettings` in the same file that does set spaceSkipsWords: true, which is almost certainly the source of the error, but that constant is a baseline/test default and is NOT what governs the application's user settings; the operative `booleanProp("textInput.spaceSkipsWords", false)` defaults it to false.)

The rest of the claim is CONFIRMED by source (packages/keybr-textinput/lib/textinput.ts):
- stopOnError default true: when set, errors do not accumulate as visible 'garbage' and the cursor does not advance past an error (`if (!this.stopOnError) { chars.push(...garbage) }`); it pauses rather than hard-stopping/ending the lesson. A lesson is never terminated by an error — appendChar returns Feedback.Failed/Recovered/Succeeded, never an 'end lesson' signal.
- forgiveErrors default true, with 3-char lookahead recovery: `const recoverBufferLength = 3;`. Two recovery routines, both gated on forgiveErrors: `#handleReplacedCharacter()` (single wrong/substituted char) and `#handleSkippedCharacter()` (single omitted char). Each checks that the next 3 characters (recoverBufferLength) of the buffered 'garbage' match the expected upcoming text; if so it commits a typo step plus the buffered correct steps and returns Feedback.Recovered. So 'auto-recovers a single wrong/skipped char via 3-char lookahead' is accurate. Note: error buffering that enables this recovery happens when `!stopOnError || forgiveErrors` is true; garbage buffer is capped at garbageBufferLength = 10.</correctedStatement>
<parameter name="evidence">Primary source, packages/keybr-textinput/lib/settings.ts (master branch, HTTP 200): The user-default props are `export const textInputProps = { stopOnError: booleanProp("textInput.stopOnError", true), forgiveErrors: booleanProp("textInput.forgiveErrors", true), spaceSkipsWords: booleanProp("textInput.spaceSkipsWords", false), } as const;` — note spaceSkipsWords default is FALSE. The function `toTextInputSettings(settings)` reads exactly these three props, so this is the operative default. A separate constant in the same file, `export const textInputSettings = { stopOnError: true, forgiveErrors: true, spaceSkipsWords: true } as const satisfies TextInputSettings;`, does set spaceSkipsWords true — this is the likely source of the claim's error but it is not the user-facing default.

Primary source, packages/keybr-textinput/lib/textinput.ts (master branch, HTTP 200): `const recoverBufferLength = 3; const garbageBufferLength = 10;`. In appendChar, error handling: `this.#typo = true; if (!this.stopOnError || this.forgiveErrors) { if (this.#garbage.length < garbageBufferLength) { this.#garbage.push(...) } } if (this.forgiveErrors && (this.#handleReplacedCharacter() || this.#handleSkippedCharacter())) { return this.#return(Feedback.Recovered); } else { return this.#return(Feedback.Failed); }`. `#handleReplacedCharacter()` requires `this.#garbage.length >= recoverBufferLength + 1` and loops `for (let i = 0; i < recoverBufferLength; i++) { if (this.at(this.pos+i+1).codePoint !== this.#garbage[i+1].codePoint) return false; }`. `#handleSkippedCharacter()` requires `this.#garbage.length >= recoverBufferLength` and loops checking `this.at(this.pos+i+1).codePoint !== this.#garbage[i].codePoint`. stopOnError controls display: `#update()` does `if (!this.stopOnError) { chars.push(...this.#garbage.map(({char}) => char)); }`. Feedback enum = { Succeeded, Recovered, Failed } — there is no 'lesson ended' feedback, confirming an error never hard-stops/ends the lesson; the cursor stays at the unmet position until the correct key.

claim: The Profile/Analysis page's percentile 'beats X% of all other people' benchmark is computed against a PRECOMPUTED static empirical distribution of all users (dist_speed.json / dist_accuracy.json baked into the frontend, smoothed 3x and bucketized), via distribution.cdf(value) — not a live server query of current users.
correctedStatement:
CONFIRMED by primary source (github.com/aradzie/keybr.com). The "beats X% of all other people" benchmark on the Profile page is computed entirely client-side against a static, baked-in empirical distribution — there is NO live/real-time server query of current users.

Mechanism (exact file paths and code):

1. STATIC DATA: Two JSON arrays of raw histogram counts are committed into the frontend source: packages/keybr-chart/lib/dist/dist_speed.json (array of 751 integers) and packages/keybr-chart/lib/dist/dist_accuracy.json (array of 1001 integers). They are imported at build time via `import speed from "./dist_speed.json"` / `import accuracy from "./dist_accuracy.json"`. Both files were last modified on 2024-10-03 (commit 417bcb4f "feat: accuracy histogram") and have never been updated since — confirming the staleness caveat: the reference population does NOT change as live users improve.

2. SMOOTHING + BUCKETIZE (packages/keybr-chart/lib/dist/dist.ts): 
   - Speed: `new Distribution(bucketize(smooth(smooth(smooth(speed, 5), 5), 5), 150))` — smoothed 3x with window 5, then bucketized into 150 buckets.
   - Accuracy: `new Distribution(smooth(smooth(smooth(accuracy, 5), 5), 5))` — smoothed 3x with window 5, but NOT bucketized (the "bucketize(…,150)" step applies to SPEED ONLY, not accuracy).
   The smooth() function (util.ts) is a centered moving average; bucketize() (util.ts) averages values within each of N buckets — note buckets=150, not bucketSize=150.

3. CDF LOOKUP: packages/keybr-math/lib/dist.ts defines class Distribution (documented as an "Empirical distribution function"). Its constructor builds a normalized PMF and a running-sum CDF. In SpeedHistogramSection.tsx the percentile is `distribution.cdf(value)` where value = stats.speed.avg (or stats.speed.max for "top"). In AccuracyHistogramSection.tsx it is `distribution.cdf(distribution.scale(value))` (accuracy scales the [0,1] value to a histogram index first). The result is rendered via FormattedMessage id "profile.chart.compareAverageSpeed.description" with exact default text: "Your all time average speed beats {value} of all other people." (and analogous compareTopSpeed / accuracy variants).

4. NO NETWORK: grep of the entire data path (dist.ts, index.ts, SpeedHistogramSection.tsx, AccuracyHistogramSection.tsx) found zero fetch/axios/http/await/async calls. The distribution is built with `useMemo(() => makeSpeedDistribution(), [])` purely in-browser.

Minor corrections to the original claim wording: (a) the triple-smoothing uses window=5 each pass [smooth(...,5)x3]; (b) bucketize(…,150) means 150 buckets and applies to SPEED ONLY — accuracy is smoothed but not bucketized; (c) accuracy additionally applies distribution.scale(value) before cdf(). Otherwise the claim is accurate in every substantive respect, including the staleness implication.
evidence:
packages/keybr-chart/lib/dist/dist.ts:
  export function makeSpeedDistribution(): Distribution { return new Distribution(bucketize(smooth(smooth(smooth(speed, 5), 5), 5), 150)); }
  export function makeAccuracyDistribution(): Distribution { return new Distribution(smooth(smooth(smooth(accuracy, 5), 5), 5)); }
  imports: `import accuracy from "./dist_accuracy.json"`, `import speed from "./dist_speed.json"`.

packages/keybr-chart/lib/dist/util.ts: smooth(data, wnd) = centered moving average over [i-wnd, i+wnd]; bucketize(data, buckets) averages into `buckets` groups (bucketSize = data.length/buckets).

packages/keybr-math/lib/dist.ts: class Distribution implements Iterable<Sample>; "@see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empirical_distribution_function"; constructor computes #pmf[i]=samples[i]/totalSum and #cdf[i]=runningSum; cdf(index) rounds index, returns 0 if <0, 1 if > length-1, else #cdf[index].

packages/page-profile/lib/profile/SpeedHistogramSection.tsx: `const distribution = useMemo(() => makeSpeedDistribution(), []); const value = period === "top" ? stats.speed.max : stats.speed.avg; const cdf = distribution.cdf(value);` rendered as FormattedMessage defaultMessage="Your all time average speed beats {value} of all other people." Description: "This is a histogram of the typing speeds of all users, and your position in relation to them."

packages/page-profile/lib/profile/AccuracyHistogramSection.tsx: `const distribution = useMemo(() => makeAccuracyDistribution(), []); const cdf = distribution.cdf(distribution.scale(value));` defaultMessage="Your all time average accuracy beats {value} of all other people."

Data: dist_speed.json = array of 751 integers (raw counts, e.g. [0,0,...,1,1,2,2,3,...]); dist_accuracy.json = array of 1001 integers. Both last touched 2024-10-03 (commit 417bcb4f), never since. No fetch/http/async anywhere in the data path.

claim: Storage is local-first: anonymous (not-signed-in) users' results are stored ONLY in browser IndexedDB (object store 'history'), settings in a local 'settings' object store; signing in adds server sync to /_/sync/data (binary octet-stream) and a per-user binary file stats.data, with the remote load path treating server data as source-of-truth and NO client/server merge logic.
correctedStatement:
keybr.com (open source, github.com/aradzie/keybr.com, by Aleksei Radzievski) is local-first for anonymous users, and the core architecture in the claim is correct, with two precision corrections about WHERE settings are stored.

CONFIRMED:
1. Anonymous-user RESULTS are stored only locally in browser IndexedDB. In packages/keybr-result-loader/lib/internal/local.ts, `PersistentResultStorage` uses `new DBNamedFactory(indexedDB, "history", 1)`. The IndexedDB database is named "history" and its single object store is also named "history" (the migration does `db.createObjectStore(db.name, { autoIncrement: true })`, and db.name === "history"). Results are written via `store.add(resultToJson(result))` — i.e. stored as JSON, NOT binary, in the local DB. IndexedDB is used in exactly ONE place repo-wide (only local.ts / its idb.ts wrapper); a repo code search for `indexedDB` confirms no other production use.

2. Sign-in adds server sync. openResultStorage (storage.ts) returns `ResultStorageOfAnonymousUser(local)` when userId == null, and `ResultStorageOfNamedUser(local, remote)` with `remote = new ResultSyncNamedUser()` when signed in.

3. The sync endpoint is `/_/sync/data` and the wire format is binary application/octet-stream. In remotesync.ts: `const url = "/_/sync/data"`; receive() does `request.use(expectType("application/octet-stream")).GET(path)` then `parseFile(new Uint8Array(await response.arrayBuffer()))`; send() POSTs `formatMessage(results)` with content type "application/octet-stream". Server controller (packages/server/lib/app/sync/controller.ts) maps GET/POST/DELETE /_/sync/data (and GET /_/sync/data/{id} for public users) to a UserDataFactory.

4. The per-user server file is served as a binary attachment named stats.data. In packages/keybr-result-userdata/lib/userdata.ts, UserData.serve() sets `ctx.response.type = "application/octet-stream"` and `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="stats.data"`. On disk it lives at DataDir.userStatsFile(userId) (under user_stats/...). The file content is the keybr binary result format (fileHeader/fileChunk/parseFile).

5. NO client/server merge; server is source of truth. This is now HIGH confidence (the original flag downgraded it to medium). ResultStorageOfNamedUser.load() first calls remote.receive(); if it returns any results it returns them directly (ignoring local). Only if remote is empty does it load local, upload it to remote (send), clear local, and return it — a one-time migration of anonymous data on first sign-in. This is explicitly asserted by the official unit test in storage.test.ts, including a test literally named "named user - fetch remote and ignore local data" (asserts results == remote, local unmodified) and "named user - upload local to remote on first sync" (asserts local is cleared after upload). There is no merge step. receive() just parses the server file.

CORRECTIONS:
A. SETTINGS are NOT stored in an IndexedDB 'settings' object store. Client settings use browser localStorage under the key "settings" (packages/keybr-settings-loader/lib/internal: STORAGE_KEY = "settings"; ObjectStorage wraps `localStorage` by default and uses setItem/getItem with JSON.stringify). The phrase "local 'settings' object store" is imprecise/misleading because "object store" implies IndexedDB; settings never touch IndexedDB. (Server-side signed-in settings are stored as a per-user JSON file via SettingsDatabase at DataDir.userSettingsFile(userId), synced over a SEPARATE endpoint PUT /_/sync/settings as JSON — not /_/sync/data and not octet-stream.)
B. Minor: the local IndexedDB results are stored as JSON (resultToJson), whereas the server stats.data file and the /_/sync/data wire format are binary. The claim's word "binary" applies to the server side and sync transport, not to the local IndexedDB results.

So: results-in-IndexedDB-'history', /_/sync/data octet-stream, stats.data binary file, and the server-as-source-of-truth-with-no-merge behavior are all CONFIRMED (the no-merge point at high confidence, backed by code and named tests). The only inaccuracy is calling the local settings store an "object store" — it is localStorage keyed "settings", not IndexedDB.
evidence:
Primary source: github.com/aradzie/keybr.com (master), read via GitHub API.

(1) packages/keybr-result-loader/lib/internal/local.ts: `export class PersistentResultStorage implements LocalResultStorage { readonly #factory = new DBNamedFactory(indexedDB, "history", 1); ... }` with `store.add(resultToJson(result))` and migration `db.createObjectStore(db.name, { autoIncrement: true })` (db.name === "history"). types.ts comments LocalResultStorage as "keeps results in a local indexed DB".

(2) packages/keybr-result-loader/lib/internal/storage.ts, openRawResultStorage: private + userId==null -> `new ResultStorageOfAnonymousUser(new PersistentResultStorage())`; private + userId!=null -> `new ResultStorageOfNamedUser(new PersistentResultStorage(), new ResultSyncNamedUser())`. ResultStorageOfNamedUser.load(): `const results = await this.#remote.receive(pl); if (results.length > 0) { return results; } else { const results = await this.#local.load(); if (results.length > 0) { await this.#remote.send(results, pl); await this.#local.clear(); return results; } } return [];` — no merge.

(3) packages/keybr-result-loader/lib/internal/remotesync.ts: `const url = "/_/sync/data";` receive() uses `expectType("application/octet-stream")` + `parseFile(new Uint8Array(await response.arrayBuffer()))`; send() POSTs `formatMessage(results)` as `"application/octet-stream"`.

(4) packages/keybr-result-userdata/lib/userdata.ts: `ctx.response.type = "application/octet-stream"; ctx.response.headers.set("Content-Disposition", 'attachment; filename="stats.data"');` File parsed/written with parseFile/fileHeader/fileChunk (binary).

(5) packages/server/lib/app/sync/controller.ts: `@http.GET("/_/sync/data")`, `@http.POST("/_/sync/data")` (`@body.binary` maxLength 1048576), `@http.DELETE("/_/sync/data")`, plus `@http.GET("/_/sync/data/{id:[a-zA-Z0-9]+}")` -> `this.userData.load(...).serve(ctx)` / `.append(...)` / `.delete()`.

(6) packages/keybr-result-loader/lib/internal/storage.test.ts: test "named user - fetch remote and ignore local data" -> `deepEqual(results, [r2, r3]); deepEqual(local, [r0, r1]);` (returns remote, local untouched). test "named user - upload local to remote on first sync" -> `deepEqual(results, [r0, r1]); deepEqual(local, []); deepEqual(remote, [r0, r1]);` (local uploaded then cleared).

SETTINGS correction: packages/keybr-settings-loader/lib/internal/storage.ts: `export const STORAGE_KEY = "settings";` anonymous path uses `storage.get(STORAGE_KEY)` / `storage.set(STORAGE_KEY, settings.toJSON())`; signed-in path PUTs to `/_/sync/settings` with `settings.toJSON()` (JSON). packages/keybr-settings-loader/lib/internal/objectstore.ts: `class ObjectStorage { constructor(storage: Storage = localStorage) { this.#storage = storage } ... setItem/getItem with JSON.stringify/JSON.parse }` — localStorage, not IndexedDB. Server-side: packages/keybr-settings-database/lib/index.ts writes per-user JSON file at DataDir.userSettingsFile(userId) (datadir.ts). Repo-wide code search for `indexedDB` returns only keybr-result-loader (local.ts/idb.ts) + test-env mock — confirming settings never use IndexedDB.

claim: Essentially the ENTIRE statistics/analytics feature set (all charts, per-key stats, percentile histograms, predictive lessons-remaining, data export) is FREE; Premium is a one-time lifetime (non-subscription) Paddle payment that only removes ads/trackers and speeds page loads — no learning/analysis feature is paywalled.
correctedStatement: CONFIRMED with one minor caveat about "data export." Primary evidence from the open-source frontend (github.com/aradzie/keybr.com, AGPL-3.0) shows: (1) The premium account ONLY removes ads/trackers and speeds page loads, and is a one-time lifetime payment via Paddle, not a subscription. The user-facing copy in packages/page-account/lib/AccountSection.tsx states verbatim: "No ads. Ads may be distracting and impede your learning progress." / "No trackers. Trackers inevitably come with ads. Remove all trackers for complete online privacy." / "Ultra-fast responsiveness. Ads take quite some time to load. Getting rid of them means faster loading times." and "It is a single time payment that provides lifetime access. It is NOT a recurring subscription." Premium status is a single boolean field — `readonly premium: boolean` on the NamedUser type (packages/keybr-pages-shared/lib/types.ts), set by a "Premium account order" (OrderDetails). (2) NO learning/analysis feature is paywalled. The helper `isPremiumUser` is imported ONLY in AccountSection.tsx, where it toggles ads-related account messaging — nothing else. The profile/analytics page (packages/page-profile/lib/ProfilePage.tsx) does NOT import or reference `isPremiumUser` or any premium flag; it renders ALL stats/charts sections unconditionally: AllTimeSummary, TodaySummary, AccuracyStreaksSection, HistogramsSection, ProgressOverviewSection, SpeedChartSection, KeySpeedChartSection, KeySpeedHistogramSection, KeyFrequencyHistogramSection, KeyFrequencyHeatmapSection, CalendarSection. The percentile histogram is confirmed free — AccuracyHistogramSection.tsx shows "a histogram of the accuracies of all users, and your position in relation to them" (a CDF) with no premium gating. Per-key stats (keybr-result/lib/keystats.ts) and predictive lessons-remaining (README: "predicts how many lessons it will take to reach the target speed") are core free features. CAVEAT ON "DATA EXPORT": I found no user-facing download/export button for personal statistics. What exists is DataScript.tsx, which embeds the user's stats (allTime/today/byDate) as JSON in a <script id="profile-data"> tag (scrapable but not a UI export), and keybr-keyboard-io/lib/export.ts which exports keyboard LAYOUTS, not stats. So "data export" overstates a deliberate export feature, though raw stats data is freely present in the page. PRICE CAVEAT: The price is NOT hardcoded — AccountPricePreview.tsx fetches it dynamically from Paddle via paddle.PricePreview(priceId), so it is region/tax-dependent. The specific ~US$14 / S$12.88 figures come only from third-party reviews and are low confidence; the source code does not confirm any specific amount.
evidence: Primary sources from the open-source frontend github.com/aradzie/keybr.com (AGPL-3.0): (1) packages/page-account/lib/AccountSection.tsx — user-facing premium copy: "No ads...", "No trackers...", "Ultra-fast responsiveness...", and "It is a single time payment that provides lifetime access. It is NOT a recurring subscription." This file is the ONLY importer of `isPremiumUser` (imported from @keybr/pages-shared), and uses it solely to switch between the "thank you for purchasing" message and the buy-prompt/benefits list. (2) packages/page-account/lib/checkout.ts + paddle/ directory + AccountPricePreview.tsx — confirms Paddle integration (overlay one-page checkout, quantity 1) and that the price is fetched dynamically via paddle.PricePreview() using paddlePriceId from @keybr/thirdparties; no hardcoded price. (3) packages/keybr-pages-shared/lib/types.ts — premium is a single `readonly premium: boolean` on NamedUser, tied to an OrderDetails "Premium account order." (4) packages/page-profile/lib/ProfilePage.tsx — renders all 11+ stats/chart sections unconditionally; does NOT import or reference isPremiumUser or any premium flag (the only conditional is publicUser.id != null for the ShareProfileLink). (5) packages/page-profile/lib/profile/AccuracyHistogramSection.tsx — percentile histogram ("histogram of the accuracies of all users, and your position in relation to them"), no premium gating. (6) packages/keybr-chart/ contains all chart/histogram components (SpeedHistogram, AccuracyHistogram, KeySpeedHistogram, KeyDetailsChart, KeyFrequencyHeatmap, ProgressOverviewChart, TimeToTypeHistogram). (7) README.md confirms per-key statistics and "predicts how many lessons it will take to reach the target speed" as core features, with no premium/payment mention. CAVEATS: "data export" overstated — only DataScript.tsx (embeds stats as inline JSON, no download UI) and keybr-keyboard-io/export.ts (layouts, not stats) exist. Price figures (~US$14/S$12.88) are third-party only and unconfirmed by source; price is region/tax-dependent via Paddle. Limitation: the live production build is not the open-source repo and could differ, but the repo is the official keybr.com frontend by the author.
